title: Container Security Quiz
description: Test your knowledge on running as non-root, minimal images, and avoiding host breakouts.

questions.question: What is the primary risk of allowing the main process in a Docker container to run as 'root'?

options.text: It consumes significantly more CPU and RAM resources.
Because containers share the host's kernel, a compromised root process in a container makes breaking out and compromising the entire host server much easier.
Docker Hub will refuse to host the image.
It prevents Kubernetes from load balancing the container properly.

questions.question: Which Dockerfile directive should be used to switch to a less-privileged assigned user right before the final `CMD` execution?

options.text: SWITCH_USER
USER
RUN_AS
PRIVILEGE

questions.question: What is the primary security benefit of utilizing Google 'Distroless' Docker images?

options.text: They automatically update themselves to patch vulnerabilities while running.
They encrypt all network traffic leaving the container via HTTPS.
They omit operating system utilities, including package managers and basic shells like bash, drastically reducing the attack surface for hackers.
They bypass Docker and execute directly on the bare-metal hardware.

questions.question: How does enforcing a 'readOnlyRootFilesystem' policy protect a cloud application?

options.text: It encrypts the database passwords so users can't read them.
It stops hackers who have breached the application from being able to download and save malware/scripts to the container's disk.
It prevents DDoS attacks by blocking incoming POST requests.
It makes the container run exclusively in the CPU cache.

questions.question: What role does an open-source tool like 'Falco' play in a Kubernetes cluster?

options.text: It provisions initial IAM roles automatically.
It is an Ingress controller used for routing internet traffic securely.
It scans static Dockerfiles for bad practices before they are built.
It acts as a runtime security monitor, interacting with the Linux Kernel to alert on suspicious behavior like unexpected shell executions inside pods.
